1: <header>Holistic Life Cycle Assessment of a biogas-based electricity generation plant in a pig farm considering co-digestion and an additive </header>
F.F. Freitas a, , A.C. Furtado J.A.V. Piñas O.J. Venturini R.M. Barros d E.E.S. Lora a

<list>a Núcleo de Excelência Em Gestão Termelétrica e Distribuída - NEST / IEM, UNIFEI, Universidade Federal de Itajubá, Av. BPS 1303, CP 50, Itajubá, MG, 37.500-903, Brazil 
b Department of Renewable Energies, UNILA, Universidade Federal de Integraçõ Latinoamericana, Av. Sílvio Américo Sasdelli, 1842, Foz Do Iguaçu, PR, Brazil 
C Universidade Federal de Uberlândia (UFU), Uberlândia, 38400-902, Minas Gerais, Brazil 
d Institute of Natural Resources (IRN), Universidade Federal de Itajubá, Av. BPS 1303, CP 50, Itajubá, MG, 37.500-903, Brazil</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Pig manure Corn silage Elephant-grass silage Biochar additive Biological process treatment|This study aims to perform an LCA of the co-digestion of pig manure with two co-substrates (corn silage and elephant grass) and an additive (biochar) to produce biogas for electricity generation. Four scenarios were defined: considering only the digestion of pig manure (Base scenario); co-digestion of pig manure with elephant grass-silage (scenario II); co-digestion of pig manure with corn silage (scenario III), and digestion of pig manure with biochar as an additive (scenario IV). The biogas produced was used for electricity generation in a cogen- eration system. The thermal energy obtained from the cogeneration system was used to heat the substrate, maintain a mesophilic digestion temperature (38 °C), and heat the pig stalls. Digestate was considered an organic fertilizer in this analysis. Scenario III presented the worst results for most of the impact categories considered in the LCA, with only a positive impact on ozone depletion (ODP). Scenario II presented a lower impact than Scenario III. Using co-substrates and additives in the digestion of pig manure has allowed a considerable increase in electricity production. However, the environmental impacts of using co-substrates are significant in most impact categories, mainly due to the use of fossil fuels in the chain of silage production.
2: Using biomass has huge and viable potential for reducing depen- dence on fossil fuels. It can also help to improve living standards in developing countries. Biomass energy can be harnessed in different ways, using thermochemical conversion processes such as combustion, pyrolysis, and gasification, or biological processes such as anaerobic digestion. Although complex, Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is a suitable and efficient technology for treating organic waste. Several acting microor- ganisms are affected by factors such as average pH, substrate type, toxic agents, temperature, and hydraulic retention time, among other things [1-3].

Biogas for generating thermal and electric energy from organic waste plays a fundamental role in environmental protection, distributed gen- eration, and rural development [4]. Waste and residues of animal and vegetable origin produce environmental problems when not properly treated. They may contaminate the water table and bodies, leading to
aquatic eutrophication and reducing oxygen availability in water and photosynthesis. All these effects result in unsafe drinking water, affecting public health.

Solid waste management must be carried out in an integrated manner and always aims to preserve natural resources and the health and safety of populations on the premises of sustainable development. This assertion is converted, for example, into actions and policies that seek to reduce net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The intrinsic characteristics of each project are its integrated management steps that prioritize, for example, the transport of waste with low GHG fuels and forms of treatment and final disposal involving technologies that mini- mize GHG emissions correlated with good waste recycling and reuse techniques.

In this sense, fugitive gas emissions from landfills, non-optimized solid waste transport projects in net GHG emissions, and recycling plants based on non-renewable energy sources could affect air envi- ronmental quality. Non-renewable energy sources emit a greater amount of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2eq/kWh), and gaseous

fugitive emissions from landfills release biogas into the atmosphere, which is composed of carbon dioxide (CO2, 15-40 vol%) and methane (CH4; 35-65 vol%), a gas with great greenhouse power, in addition to water, and 1-15% of other gases including water vapor, H2S, and NH3 [5,6].

Landfills or anaerobic digestion of animal waste or residual biomass produce biogas that can be used energetically, mitigating GHG emis- sions from substituted non-renewable energy sources [5-7]. In addition, a well-designed and operated landfill can reduce fugitive biogas emis- sions. Ultimately, these gases can contribute to global warming since emissions, waste carbon footprints, and effluents treatment utilities are essential issues [8]. Therefore, solid waste management is essential in studies to reduce net GHG emissions and, subsequently, global warming [9].

Treating biomass residues for biogas generation will also produce biofertilizer as a byproduct of the digestion process, which can be used to recover nutrients in the soil. Treating organic wastes also leads to mitigating methane gas emissions by converting methane and oxygen to carbon dioxide and water through combustion (Eq. (1)). Biogenic methane gas has a pollutant potential 27.2 times higher than carbon dioxide [10,11]. In addition, heat is released because it is an exothermic reaction involving burning gas, which can be used in different ways, such as for electricity generation.

Mixing between different substrates plays a fundamental role in biogas production to balance the carbon and nitrogen levels in the organic matter SO they can stay within the optimal consumption ranges for the microorganism population, increasing methane yield. The number of different substrates to be used depends on the C/N ratio. The optimal C/N range of the substrate mixtures for biogas production must be between 20 and 30 carbon units per nitrogen unit [12,13]. Pig manure, for example, has a C/N ratio below this optimum ratio and can be used as co-substrate along with dedicated energy crops (DEC), such as corn (Zea mays) or elephant grass (Pennisetum purpureum) silage, with high C/N ratios.
DECs are generally planted for energy production by biomass com- bustion in thermoelectric plants or by using the feedstock as substrates

in anaerobic co-digestion processes [14,15]. These agricultural cultures may be used as base substrates, co-substrates, or single substrates in the digester. The most popular DECs are corn, grass, sunflowers, beets, etc. [16], which can significantly increase biogas production when used as co-substrates with pig or bovine manure (Fig. 1) [17-27].

Corn is one of the most widely available crops in the world and can be used for both human consumption and animal feed [28]. Elephant grass has been used in Brazil as fuel at thermal power plants and is being evaluated as raw material for lignocellulosic biorefineries [29]. The ensiling of corn and elephant grass leads to the production of lactic acid, which is one of the precursors to biogas formation [30-33]. A biogas production system was evaluated using co-digestion with energy crops in conjunction with pig manure. We noticed that the most critical environmental impacts correspond to the agricultural system related to the use of fossil fuels and mineral fertilizers and not to anaerobic digestion [34].

Some additives, which are substances supplied to the digester in small amounts with the substrates, can increase the efficiency of the anaerobic digestion process. Examples of additives include trace min- erals, micro and macronutrients, buffering agents, ammonia inhibition compounds, and compounds with high biomass immobilization capacity [35]. It is generally possible to increase biogas yields by up to 20%-30% using additives [36]. Different studies have shown increased biogas yields when using biochar as an additive with increases in biogas pro- duction between 5% and 35% (Fig. 2) [37-41] while also reducing problems related to inhibition [42].

Although this is a relatively novel research topic, studies have already shown promising results in this field. Inhibitory compounds in the digester are adsorbed by the biochar, promoting Direct Interspecies Electronic Transfer (DIET) and acting as microbial immobilization. The porosity of the biochar creates a surface area on which methanogenic archaea develops and adheres, reducing the inhibition of absorbent in- hibitors [43,44]. Biochar also acts as an adsorbent to support the development of a stable bacterial community [45]. Other authors have also suggested that it improves results due to the immobilization of microorganisms [42,46].

An environmental analysis can be conducted using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to compare scenarios with different substrates for energy generation from biogas. LCA is a quantitative tool for assessing the environmental impacts associated with a product or process and for identifying opportunities for environmental benefits. Several LCA studies in the literature on biogas production systems analyzed either greenhouse gas emissions or fossil fuel consumption using different raw materials (mono and co-digestion) for several applications [49-51]. The studies showed that the environmental impact of biogas systems de- pends largely on the raw material, the efficiency of the biogas produc- tion chain, the methane losses, and the technologies for converting biogas into electricity [49,52].
Methane leaks from biogas plants vary. However, most studies show losses ranging from 0.3 to 6.4% [53-57]. In this work, we assumed 3% of fugitive emissions.

Intensive livestock production in pig farming is widely considered to harm the environment [58]. This is due to the production chain that requires significant amounts of feed, energy, and water for the produc- tion of pork and releases byproducts such as methane gas (CH4), ammonia (NH3), and other emissions to air, water, and soil with the risks of pollution resulting from ineffective waste management and treatment practices [59,60].

According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) report of 2021, 122.5 million tonnes of pork meat were produced globally. In addition, FAO (2022) predicts a 2.5% increase in pork meat production in 2022, bringing this production to 125.6 million tonnes [61]. As a result, concern about the amount of carbon dioxide equivalent produced annually increases [62].
Thus, it is sought to take advantage of the waste generated in the production of swine for electricity generation through the conversion of
Fig. 1. Biogas production increase in the co-digestion process (%). Source: Adapted from [17,18,27,19-26].
Fig. 2. Increased biogas production (%) using biochar as an additive in anaerobic digestion. Source: Adapted from [37-41,47,48].

waste into biogas and its consequent conversion into electricity. Therefore, an environmental liability concerning porcine waste is offset, and electrical energy consumed from the grid, partially derived from fossil fuels, is replaced. The tool for environmental modeling of complex systems is used through the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to verify this pork production model's positive and negative impacts on the energy use of porcine waste.

The study seeks to conduct LCA on different substrates in mono- digestion and co-digestion. Four scenarios were evaluated using pig manure, corn silage, and elephant grass silage to generate electricity and supply it to the electricity grid. One of the scenarios also uses biochar as an additive. The first scenario uses only pig manure in the mono- digestion configuration. The second uses pig manure in co-digestion with elephant grass silage. The third uses pig manure in co-digestion with corn silage. The fourth scenario uses pig manure with biochar as

an additive. The main novelty of this paper is the LCA data generated from a theoretical evaluation of potential increases in biogas and electricity production through co-digestion and additives in a large-scale pig farm based on the generalization of laboratory results.

Using biomass has huge and viable potential for reducing depen- dence on fossil fuels. It can also help to improve living standards in developing countries. Biomass energy can be harnessed in different ways, using thermochemical conversion processes such as combustion, pyrolysis, and gasification, or biological processes such as anaerobic digestion. Although complex, Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is a suitable and efficient technology for treating organic waste. Several acting microor- ganisms are affected by factors such as average pH, substrate type, toxic agents, temperature, and hydraulic retention time, among other things [1-3].

Biogas for generating thermal and electric energy from organic waste plays a fundamental role in environmental protection, distributed gen- eration, and rural development [4]. Waste and residues of animal and vegetable origin produce environmental problems when not properly treated. They may contaminate the water table and bodies, leading to
aquatic eutrophication and reducing oxygen availability in water and photosynthesis. All these effects result in unsafe drinking water, affecting public health.

Solid waste management must be carried out in an integrated manner and always aims to preserve natural resources and the health and safety of populations on the premises of sustainable development. This assertion is converted, for example, into actions and policies that seek to reduce net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The intrinsic characteristics of each project are its integrated management steps that prioritize, for example, the transport of waste with low GHG fuels and forms of treatment and final disposal involving technologies that mini- mize GHG emissions correlated with good waste recycling and reuse techniques.

In this sense, fugitive gas emissions from landfills, non-optimized solid waste transport projects in net GHG emissions, and recycling plants based on non-renewable energy sources could affect air envi- ronmental quality. Non-renewable energy sources emit a greater amount of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2eq/kWh), and gaseous

fugitive emissions from landfills release biogas into the atmosphere, which is composed of carbon dioxide (CO2, 15-40 vol%) and methane (CH4; 35-65 vol%), a gas with great greenhouse power, in addition to water, and 1-15% of other gases including water vapor, H2S, and NH3 [5,6].

Landfills or anaerobic digestion of animal waste or residual biomass produce biogas that can be used energetically, mitigating GHG emis- sions from substituted non-renewable energy sources [5-7]. In addition, a well-designed and operated landfill can reduce fugitive biogas emis- sions. Ultimately, these gases can contribute to global warming since emissions, waste carbon footprints, and effluents treatment utilities are essential issues [8]. Therefore, solid waste management is essential in studies to reduce net GHG emissions and, subsequently, global warming [9].

Treating biomass residues for biogas generation will also produce biofertilizer as a byproduct of the digestion process, which can be used to recover nutrients in the soil. Treating organic wastes also leads to mitigating methane gas emissions by converting methane and oxygen to carbon dioxide and water through combustion (Eq. (1)). Biogenic methane gas has a pollutant potential 27.2 times higher than carbon dioxide [10,11]. In addition, heat is released because it is an exothermic reaction involving burning gas, which can be used in different ways, such as for electricity generation.

Mixing between different substrates plays a fundamental role in biogas production to balance the carbon and nitrogen levels in the organic matter SO they can stay within the optimal consumption ranges for the microorganism population, increasing methane yield. The number of different substrates to be used depends on the C/N ratio. The optimal C/N range of the substrate mixtures for biogas production must be between 20 and 30 carbon units per nitrogen unit [12,13]. Pig manure, for example, has a C/N ratio below this optimum ratio and can be used as co-substrate along with dedicated energy crops (DEC), such as corn (Zea mays) or elephant grass (Pennisetum purpureum) silage, with high C/N ratios.
DECs are generally planted for energy production by biomass com- bustion in thermoelectric plants or by using the feedstock as substrates

in anaerobic co-digestion processes [14,15]. These agricultural cultures may be used as base substrates, co-substrates, or single substrates in the digester. The most popular DECs are corn, grass, sunflowers, beets, etc. [16], which can significantly increase biogas production when used as co-substrates with pig or bovine manure (Fig. 1) [17-27].

Corn is one of the most widely available crops in the world and can be used for both human consumption and animal feed [28]. Elephant grass has been used in Brazil as fuel at thermal power plants and is being evaluated as raw material for lignocellulosic biorefineries [29]. The ensiling of corn and elephant grass leads to the production of lactic acid, which is one of the precursors to biogas formation [30-33]. A biogas production system was evaluated using co-digestion with energy crops in conjunction with pig manure. We noticed that the most critical environmental impacts correspond to the agricultural system related to the use of fossil fuels and mineral fertilizers and not to anaerobic digestion [34].

Some additives, which are substances supplied to the digester in small amounts with the substrates, can increase the efficiency of the anaerobic digestion process. Examples of additives include trace min- erals, micro and macronutrients, buffering agents, ammonia inhibition compounds, and compounds with high biomass immobilization capacity [35]. It is generally possible to increase biogas yields by up to 20%-30% using additives [36]. Different studies have shown increased biogas yields when using biochar as an additive with increases in biogas pro- duction between 5% and 35% (Fig. 2) [37-41] while also reducing problems related to inhibition [42].

Although this is a relatively novel research topic, studies have already shown promising results in this field. Inhibitory compounds in the digester are adsorbed by the biochar, promoting Direct Interspecies Electronic Transfer (DIET) and acting as microbial immobilization. The porosity of the biochar creates a surface area on which methanogenic archaea develops and adheres, reducing the inhibition of absorbent in- hibitors [43,44]. Biochar also acts as an adsorbent to support the development of a stable bacterial community [45]. Other authors have also suggested that it improves results due to the immobilization of microorganisms [42,46].

An environmental analysis can be conducted using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to compare scenarios with different substrates for energy generation from biogas. LCA is a quantitative tool for assessing the environmental impacts associated with a product or process and for identifying opportunities for environmental benefits. Several LCA studies in the literature on biogas production systems analyzed either greenhouse gas emissions or fossil fuel consumption using different raw materials (mono and co-digestion) for several applications [49-51]. The studies showed that the environmental impact of biogas systems de- pends largely on the raw material, the efficiency of the biogas produc- tion chain, the methane losses, and the technologies for converting biogas into electricity [49,52].
Methane leaks from biogas plants vary. However, most studies show losses ranging from 0.3 to 6.4% [53-57]. In this work, we assumed 3% of fugitive emissions.

Intensive livestock production in pig farming is widely considered to harm the environment [58]. This is due to the production chain that requires significant amounts of feed, energy, and water for the produc- tion of pork and releases byproducts such as methane gas (CH4), ammonia (NH3), and other emissions to air, water, and soil with the risks of pollution resulting from ineffective waste management and treatment practices [59,60].

According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) report of 2021, 122.5 million tonnes of pork meat were produced globally. In addition, FAO (2022) predicts a 2.5% increase in pork meat production in 2022, bringing this production to 125.6 million tonnes [61]. As a result, concern about the amount of carbon dioxide equivalent produced annually increases [62].
Thus, it is sought to take advantage of the waste generated in the production of swine for electricity generation through the conversion of
Author(s)
Fig. 1. Biogas production increase in the co-digestion process (%). Source: Adapted from [17,18,27,19-26].
Author(s)
Fig. 2. Increased biogas production (%) using biochar as an additive in anaerobic digestion. Source: Adapted from [37-41,47,48].

waste into biogas and its consequent conversion into electricity. Therefore, an environmental liability concerning porcine waste is offset, and electrical energy consumed from the grid, partially derived from fossil fuels, is replaced. The tool for environmental modeling of complex systems is used through the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to verify this pork production model's positive and negative impacts on the energy use of porcine waste.

The study seeks to conduct LCA on different substrates in mono- digestion and co-digestion. Four scenarios were evaluated using pig manure, corn silage, and elephant grass silage to generate electricity and supply it to the electricity grid. One of the scenarios also uses biochar as an additive. The first scenario uses only pig manure in the mono- digestion configuration. The second uses pig manure in co-digestion with elephant grass silage. The third uses pig manure in co-digestion with corn silage. The fourth scenario uses pig manure with biochar as

an additive. The main novelty of this paper is the LCA data generated from a theoretical evaluation of potential increases in biogas and electricity production through co-digestion and additives in a large-scale pig farm based on the generalization of laboratory results.

fugitive emissions from landfills release biogas into the atmosphere, which is composed of carbon dioxide (CO2, 15-40 vol%) and methane (CH4; 35-65 vol%), a gas with great greenhouse power, in addition to water, and 1-15% of other gases including water vapor, H2S, and NH3 [5,6].

Landfills or anaerobic digestion of animal waste or residual biomass produce biogas that can be used energetically, mitigating GHG emis- sions from substituted non-renewable energy sources [5-7]. In addition, a well-designed and operated landfill can reduce fugitive biogas emis- sions. Ultimately, these gases can contribute to global warming since emissions, waste carbon footprints, and effluents treatment utilities are essential issues [8]. Therefore, solid waste management is essential in studies to reduce net GHG emissions and, subsequently, global warming [9].

Treating biomass residues for biogas generation will also produce biofertilizer as a byproduct of the digestion process, which can be used to recover nutrients in the soil. Treating organic wastes also leads to mitigating methane gas emissions by converting methane and oxygen to carbon dioxide and water through combustion (Eq. (1)). Biogenic methane gas has a pollutant potential 27.2 times higher than carbon dioxide [10,11]. In addition, heat is released because it is an exothermic reaction involving burning gas, which can be used in different ways, such as for electricity generation.

Mixing between different substrates plays a fundamental role in biogas production to balance the carbon and nitrogen levels in the organic matter SO they can stay within the optimal consumption ranges for the microorganism population, increasing methane yield. The number of different substrates to be used depends on the C/N ratio. The optimal C/N range of the substrate mixtures for biogas production must be between 20 and 30 carbon units per nitrogen unit [12,13]. Pig manure, for example, has a C/N ratio below this optimum ratio and can be used as co-substrate along with dedicated energy crops (DEC), such as corn (Zea mays) or elephant grass (Pennisetum purpureum) silage, with high C/N ratios.
DECs are generally planted for energy production by biomass com- bustion in thermoelectric plants or by using the feedstock as substrates

in anaerobic co-digestion processes [14,15]. These agricultural cultures may be used as base substrates, co-substrates, or single substrates in the digester. The most popular DECs are corn, grass, sunflowers, beets, etc. [16], which can significantly increase biogas production when used as co-substrates with pig or bovine manure (Fig. 1) [17-27].

Corn is one of the most widely available crops in the world and can be used for both human consumption and animal feed [28]. Elephant grass has been used in Brazil as fuel at thermal power plants and is being evaluated as raw material for lignocellulosic biorefineries [29]. The ensiling of corn and elephant grass leads to the production of lactic acid, which is one of the precursors to biogas formation [30-33]. A biogas production system was evaluated using co-digestion with energy crops in conjunction with pig manure. We noticed that the most critical environmental impacts correspond to the agricultural system related to the use of fossil fuels and mineral fertilizers and not to anaerobic digestion [34].

Some additives, which are substances supplied to the digester in small amounts with the substrates, can increase the efficiency of the anaerobic digestion process. Examples of additives include trace min- erals, micro and macronutrients, buffering agents, ammonia inhibition compounds, and compounds with high biomass immobilization capacity [35]. It is generally possible to increase biogas yields by up to 20%-30% using additives [36]. Different studies have shown increased biogas yields when using biochar as an additive with increases in biogas pro- duction between 5% and 35% (Fig. 2) [37-41] while also reducing problems related to inhibition [42].

Although this is a relatively novel research topic, studies have already shown promising results in this field. Inhibitory compounds in the digester are adsorbed by the biochar, promoting Direct Interspecies Electronic Transfer (DIET) and acting as microbial immobilization. The porosity of the biochar creates a surface area on which methanogenic archaea develops and adheres, reducing the inhibition of absorbent in- hibitors [43,44]. Biochar also acts as an adsorbent to support the development of a stable bacterial community [45]. Other authors have also suggested that it improves results due to the immobilization of microorganisms [42,46].

An environmental analysis can be conducted using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to compare scenarios with different substrates for energy generation from biogas. LCA is a quantitative tool for assessing the environmental impacts associated with a product or process and for identifying opportunities for environmental benefits. Several LCA studies in the literature on biogas production systems analyzed either greenhouse gas emissions or fossil fuel consumption using different raw materials (mono and co-digestion) for several applications [49-51]. The studies showed that the environmental impact of biogas systems de- pends largely on the raw material, the efficiency of the biogas produc- tion chain, the methane losses, and the technologies for converting biogas into electricity [49,52].
Methane leaks from biogas plants vary. However, most studies show losses ranging from 0.3 to 6.4% [53-57]. In this work, we assumed 3% of fugitive emissions.

Intensive livestock production in pig farming is widely considered to harm the environment [58]. This is due to the production chain that requires significant amounts of feed, energy, and water for the produc- tion of pork and releases byproducts such as methane gas (CH4), ammonia (NH3), and other emissions to air, water, and soil with the risks of pollution resulting from ineffective waste management and treatment practices [59,60].

According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) report of 2021, 122.5 million tonnes of pork meat were produced globally. In addition, FAO (2022) predicts a 2.5% increase in pork meat production in 2022, bringing this production to 125.6 million tonnes [61]. As a result, concern about the amount of carbon dioxide equivalent produced annually increases [62].
Thus, it is sought to take advantage of the waste generated in the production of swine for electricity generation through the conversion of
Author(s)
Fig. 1. Biogas production increase in the co-digestion process (%). Source: Adapted from [17,18,27,19-26].
Author(s)
Fig. 2. Increased biogas production (%) using biochar as an additive in anaerobic digestion. Source: Adapted from [37-41,47,48].

waste into biogas and its consequent conversion into electricity. Therefore, an environmental liability concerning porcine waste is offset, and electrical energy consumed from the grid, partially derived from fossil fuels, is replaced. The tool for environmental modeling of complex systems is used through the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to verify this pork production model's positive and negative impacts on the energy use of porcine waste.

The study seeks to conduct LCA on different substrates in mono- digestion and co-digestion. Four scenarios were evaluated using pig manure, corn silage, and elephant grass silage to generate electricity and supply it to the electricity grid. One of the scenarios also uses biochar as an additive. The first scenario uses only pig manure in the mono- digestion configuration. The second uses pig manure in co-digestion with elephant grass silage. The third uses pig manure in co-digestion with corn silage. The fourth scenario uses pig manure with biochar as

an additive. The main novelty of this paper is the LCA data generated from a theoretical evaluation of potential increases in biogas and electricity production through co-digestion and additives in a large-scale pig farm based on the generalization of laboratory results.
9: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Using biomass has huge and viable potential for reducing depen- dence on fossil fuels. It can also help to improve living standards in developing countries. Biomass energy can be harnessed in different ways, using thermochemical conversion processes such as combustion, pyrolysis, and gasification, or biological processes such as anaerobic digestion. Although complex, Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is a suitable and efficient technology for treating organic waste. Several acting microor- ganisms are affected by factors such as average pH, substrate type, toxic agents, temperature, and hydraulic retention time, among other things [1-3].

Biogas for generating thermal and electric energy from organic waste plays a fundamental role in environmental protection, distributed gen- eration, and rural development [4]. Waste and residues of animal and vegetable origin produce environmental problems when not properly treated. They may contaminate the water table and bodies, leading to
aquatic eutrophication and reducing oxygen availability in water and photosynthesis. All these effects result in unsafe drinking water, affecting public health.

Solid waste management must be carried out in an integrated manner and always aims to preserve natural resources and the health and safety of populations on the premises of sustainable development. This assertion is converted, for example, into actions and policies that seek to reduce net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The intrinsic characteristics of each project are its integrated management steps that prioritize, for example, the transport of waste with low GHG fuels and forms of treatment and final disposal involving technologies that mini- mize GHG emissions correlated with good waste recycling and reuse techniques.

In this sense, fugitive gas emissions from landfills, non-optimized solid waste transport projects in net GHG emissions, and recycling plants based on non-renewable energy sources could affect air envi- ronmental quality. Non-renewable energy sources emit a greater amount of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2eq/kWh), and gaseous

Mixing between different substrates plays a fundamental role in biogas production to balance the carbon and nitrogen levels in the organic matter SO they can stay within the optimal consumption ranges for the microorganism population, increasing methane yield. The number of different substrates to be used depends on the C/N ratio. The optimal C/N range of the substrate mixtures for biogas production must be between 20 and 30 carbon units per nitrogen unit [12,13]. Pig manure, for example, has a C/N ratio below this optimum ratio and can be used as co-substrate along with dedicated energy crops (DEC), such as corn (Zea mays) or elephant grass (Pennisetum purpureum) silage, with high C/N ratios.
DECs are generally planted for energy production by biomass com- bustion in thermoelectric plants or by using the feedstock as substrates

in anaerobic co-digestion processes [14,15]. These agricultural cultures may be used as base substrates, co-substrates, or single substrates in the digester. The most popular DECs are corn, grass, sunflowers, beets, etc. [16], which can significantly increase biogas production when used as co-substrates with pig or bovine manure (Fig. 1) [17-27].

Corn is one of the most widely available crops in the world and can be used for both human consumption and animal feed [28]. Elephant grass has been used in Brazil as fuel at thermal power plants and is being evaluated as raw material for lignocellulosic biorefineries [29]. The ensiling of corn and elephant grass leads to the production of lactic acid, which is one of the precursors to biogas formation [30-33]. A biogas production system was evaluated using co-digestion with energy crops in conjunction with pig manure. We noticed that the most critical environmental impacts correspond to the agricultural system related to the use of fossil fuels and mineral fertilizers and not to anaerobic digestion [34].

Some additives, which are substances supplied to the digester in small amounts with the substrates, can increase the efficiency of the anaerobic digestion process. Examples of additives include trace min- erals, micro and macronutrients, buffering agents, ammonia inhibition compounds, and compounds with high biomass immobilization capacity [35]. It is generally possible to increase biogas yields by up to 20%-30% using additives [36]. Different studies have shown increased biogas yields when using biochar as an additive with increases in biogas pro- duction between 5% and 35% (Fig. 2) [37-41] while also reducing problems related to inhibition [42].

Although this is a relatively novel research topic, studies have already shown promising results in this field. Inhibitory compounds in the digester are adsorbed by the biochar, promoting Direct Interspecies Electronic Transfer (DIET) and acting as microbial immobilization. The porosity of the biochar creates a surface area on which methanogenic archaea develops and adheres, reducing the inhibition of absorbent in- hibitors [43,44]. Biochar also acts as an adsorbent to support the development of a stable bacterial community [45]. Other authors have also suggested that it improves results due to the immobilization of microorganisms [42,46].

An environmental analysis can be conducted using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to compare scenarios with different substrates for energy generation from biogas. LCA is a quantitative tool for assessing the environmental impacts associated with a product or process and for identifying opportunities for environmental benefits. Several LCA studies in the literature on biogas production systems analyzed either greenhouse gas emissions or fossil fuel consumption using different raw materials (mono and co-digestion) for several applications [49-51]. The studies showed that the environmental impact of biogas systems de- pends largely on the raw material, the efficiency of the biogas produc- tion chain, the methane losses, and the technologies for converting biogas into electricity [49,52].
Methane leaks from biogas plants vary. However, most studies show losses ranging from 0.3 to 6.4% [53-57]. In this work, we assumed 3% of fugitive emissions.

Intensive livestock production in pig farming is widely considered to harm the environment [58]. This is due to the production chain that requires significant amounts of feed, energy, and water for the produc- tion of pork and releases byproducts such as methane gas (CH4), ammonia (NH3), and other emissions to air, water, and soil with the risks of pollution resulting from ineffective waste management and treatment practices [59,60].

According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) report of 2021, 122.5 million tonnes of pork meat were produced globally. In addition, FAO (2022) predicts a 2.5% increase in pork meat production in 2022, bringing this production to 125.6 million tonnes [61]. As a result, concern about the amount of carbon dioxide equivalent produced annually increases [62].
Thus, it is sought to take advantage of the waste generated in the production of swine for electricity generation through the conversion of

waste into biogas and its consequent conversion into electricity. Therefore, an environmental liability concerning porcine waste is offset, and electrical energy consumed from the grid, partially derived from fossil fuels, is replaced. The tool for environmental modeling of complex systems is used through the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to verify this pork production model's positive and negative impacts on the energy use of porcine waste.

The study seeks to conduct LCA on different substrates in mono- digestion and co-digestion. Four scenarios were evaluated using pig manure, corn silage, and elephant grass silage to generate electricity and supply it to the electricity grid. One of the scenarios also uses biochar as an additive. The first scenario uses only pig manure in the mono- digestion configuration. The second uses pig manure in co-digestion with elephant grass silage. The third uses pig manure in co-digestion with corn silage. The fourth scenario uses pig manure with biochar as

an additive. The main novelty of this paper is the LCA data generated from a theoretical evaluation of potential increases in biogas and electricity production through co-digestion and additives in a large-scale pig farm based on the generalization of laboratory results.
12: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Using biomass has huge and viable potential for reducing depen- dence on fossil fuels. It can also help to improve living standards in developing countries. Biomass energy can be harnessed in different ways, using thermochemical conversion processes such as combustion, pyrolysis, and gasification, or biological processes such as anaerobic digestion. Although complex, Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is a suitable and efficient technology for treating organic waste. Several acting microor- ganisms are affected by factors such as average pH, substrate type, toxic agents, temperature, and hydraulic retention time, among other things [1-3].

Biogas for generating thermal and electric energy from organic waste plays a fundamental role in environmental protection, distributed gen- eration, and rural development [4]. Waste and residues of animal and vegetable origin produce environmental problems when not properly treated. They may contaminate the water table and bodies, leading to
aquatic eutrophication and reducing oxygen availability in water and photosynthesis. All these effects result in unsafe drinking water, affecting public health.

Solid waste management must be carried out in an integrated manner and always aims to preserve natural resources and the health and safety of populations on the premises of sustainable development. This assertion is converted, for example, into actions and policies that seek to reduce net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The intrinsic characteristics of each project are its integrated management steps that prioritize, for example, the transport of waste with low GHG fuels and forms of treatment and final disposal involving technologies that mini- mize GHG emissions correlated with good waste recycling and reuse techniques.

In this sense, fugitive gas emissions from landfills, non-optimized solid waste transport projects in net GHG emissions, and recycling plants based on non-renewable energy sources could affect air envi- ronmental quality. Non-renewable energy sources emit a greater amount of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2eq/kWh), and gaseous

fugitive emissions from landfills release biogas into the atmosphere, which is composed of carbon dioxide (CO2, 15-40 vol%) and methane (CH4; 35-65 vol%), a gas with great greenhouse power, in addition to water, and 1-15% of other gases including water vapor, H2S, and NH3 [5,6].

Landfills or anaerobic digestion of animal waste or residual biomass produce biogas that can be used energetically, mitigating GHG emis- sions from substituted non-renewable energy sources [5-7]. In addition, a well-designed and operated landfill can reduce fugitive biogas emis- sions. Ultimately, these gases can contribute to global warming since emissions, waste carbon footprints, and effluents treatment utilities are essential issues [8]. Therefore, solid waste management is essential in studies to reduce net GHG emissions and, subsequently, global warming [9].

Treating biomass residues for biogas generation will also produce biofertilizer as a byproduct of the digestion process, which can be used to recover nutrients in the soil. Treating organic wastes also leads to mitigating methane gas emissions by converting methane and oxygen to carbon dioxide and water through combustion (Eq. (1)). 2: This study was carried out referencing real data obtained from a farm in the municipality of Oliveira, Minas Gerais, Brazil, which will here- after be referred to as the "reference farm." This was done by hypo- thetically implanting a pig manure co-digestion system that used different co-substrates (elephant grass silage and corn silage) and used biochar as an additive. This study was conducted following the premises and the input data of scenarios listed below (see Table 1 and Table 2):

The LCA was performed using the SimaPro 8.0 software database. In the LCA, the environmental impacts were determined considering co- digestion, additives for each scenario, and the electricity generation from biogas using a generator set. The Brazilian biogas plant modeled for the reference case of the farm in Oliveira MG was evaluated from a gate-to-gate perspective, i.e., from one defined point in the life cycle to a second defined point further along in the life cycle.

The functional unit (FU) describes how all product system inputs and outputs are calculated. This was assumed as 1 ton of pig manure in this study. The system boundaries were established to set the primary goal of converting biogas into electricity.

Some considerations were made to define the study's scope better and eliminate negligible inputs. Materials used in manufacturing and transportation of the equipment used in the production system were disregarded from the system boundaries because of their minimal im- pacts per biogas unit produced over the life cycle of the equipment [66]. The products from the biodigester system are biogas and biofertilizer, and the subproducts of biogas used in this work are electricity and heat. Biofertilizers can be used directly or after treatment, the latter falling outside of the system boundaries considered for this study. If biogas is used in cogeneration, the products are electricity and heat. In most cases, electricity is the main product.

The allocation procedure used is based on a consequential approach, also called frontier expansion, as it is considered that the digestion residue obtained substitutes mineral fertilizer and generates surplus electricity.

There is a gap in LCA methods applied to Brazil and South America, resulting from the lack of impact evaluation methodologies oriented to Brazil. Subsidies and incentives should be granted for developing these studies in this line of research.

The LCA method used in this study was the CML2 baseline 2000 v.3.01 method (Chain Management by Life Cycle Assessment) found in the SimaPro 8.0 software database. The selected impact assessment method includes the evaluation of the leading environmental impact present in biodigestion systems and is commonly used in such systems. It also has categories that reflect the significant impacts caused by elec- tricity generation from co-digestion.

Using digestion residue resulting from manure degradation is an option to reduce costs for growing raw material for substrates and avoid inappropriate disposal of nutrient-rich organic material in the environ- ment [67]. The digestion residue is considered a fertilizer by its composition and, in many cases, an effective corrective for the soil [68]. For the cultivation of corn, they evaluated the use of biofertilizer generated in the co-digestion process of pig and cattle manure. They found a similar performance to commercial fertilization with urea [69]. Several studies have also shown the effectiveness of biofertilizers pro- duced from bovine [70,71] and pig manure[72,73] for the crop of corn as a rich source of nutrients. Researchers achieved a production similar to inorganic fertilizer by applying the digestion residue of pig manure or in natura at a rate of 180 m³ a.year in the cultivation of marandu grass (Brachiaria bizantha CV. Marandu) [74]. Another study found a superior result for inorganic fertilizers by applying bovine manure biofertilizer in lettuce cultivation using digestion residue in 60 m³ /ha [75].

LCA studies on biogas production using animal manure trans- portation show that this technology is environmentally attractive since it is readily available in nature and a waste product from rearing ani- mals. When manure needs to be transported due to its low energy density and solids content, the cost and environmental impacts of the transportation stage are pretty high. An advantage of manure over other substrates is that the impacts associated with its production are allocated to primary products like meat, milk, and leather. Animal manure has more environmental benefits than waste from the food industry and municipal solid waste [76].

Greenhouse gas emissions from animals (methane from enteric digestion and carbon dioxide from respiration) are not included in the assessment since they do not result from changes in manure manage- ment [77]. Some studies exclude biofertilizer transportation and spraying [78]. The environmental impacts of building and demolishing biogas plants are comparatively small. These impacts can be further

Biofertilizers can be used directly or after treatment, the latter falling outside of the system boundaries considered for this study. If biogas is used in cogeneration, the products are electricity and heat. In most cases, electricity is the main product.

The allocation procedure used is based on a consequential approach, also called frontier expansion, as it is considered that the digestion residue obtained substitutes mineral fertilizer and generates surplus electricity.

There is a gap in LCA methods applied to Brazil and South America, resulting from the lack of impact evaluation methodologies oriented to Brazil. Subsidies and incentives should be granted for developing these studies in this line of research.

The LCA method used in this study was the CML2 baseline 2000 v.3.01 method (Chain Management by Life Cycle Assessment) found in the SimaPro 8.0 software database. The selected impact assessment method includes the evaluation of the leading environmental impact present in biodigestion systems and is commonly used in such systems. It also has categories that reflect the significant impacts caused by elec- tricity generation from co-digestion.

Using digestion residue resulting from manure degradation is an option to reduce costs for growing raw material for substrates and avoid inappropriate disposal of nutrient-rich organic material in the environ- ment [67]. The digestion residue is considered a fertilizer by its composition and, in many cases, an effective corrective for the soil [68]. For the cultivation of corn, they evaluated the use of biofertilizer generated in the co-digestion process of pig and cattle manure. They found a similar performance to commercial fertilization with urea [69]. Several studies have also shown the effectiveness of biofertilizers pro- duced from bovine [70,71] and pig manure[72,73] for the crop of corn as a rich source of nutrients. Researchers achieved a production similar to inorganic fertilizer by applying the digestion residue of pig manure or in natura at a rate of 180 m³ a.year in the cultivation of marandu grass (Brachiaria bizantha CV. Marandu) [74]. Another study found a superior result for inorganic fertilizers by applying bovine manure biofertilizer in lettuce cultivation using digestion residue in 60 m³ /ha [75].

LCA studies on biogas production using animal manure trans- portation show that this technology is environmentally attractive since it is readily available in nature and a waste product from rearing ani- mals. When manure needs to be transported due to its low energy density and solids content, the cost and environmental impacts of the transportation stage are pretty high. An advantage of manure over other substrates is that the impacts associated with its production are allocated to primary products like meat, milk, and leather. Animal manure has more environmental benefits than waste from the food industry and municipal solid waste [76].

Greenhouse gas emissions from animals (methane from enteric digestion and carbon dioxide from respiration) are not included in the assessment since they do not result from changes in manure manage- ment [77]. Some studies exclude biofertilizer transportation and spraying [78]. The environmental impacts of building and demolishing biogas plants are comparatively small. These impacts can be further

11: <header>2.3. The main proposed scenarios are </header>
i) Scenario I (base): The anaerobic digestion of pig manure. The flow- chart and boundaries of the system are shown in Fig. 3.

This Scenario used data collected from the reference farm in Oliveira MG, Brazil, complemented with data taken from literature. Regarding the use of digestion residue as a biofertilizer, literature shows that it is possible to use it as a partial or total substitute depending on the number of nutrients required by the soil. However, soil and digestion residue analyses are necessary to determine the appropriate proportions to be added SO as not to increase soil acidity or nutrient depletion (mainly N, P, and K content for energy crops).

<list>ii) Scenario II: Pig manure substrate co-digested with elephant grass silage. Fig. 4 shows the detailed schematic of the steps in this scenario. 
iii) Scenario III: Pig manure substrate co-digested with corn silage. Fig. 5 shows the detailed schematic of the steps in this scenario.</list>

The flowchart showing the system boundaries and the detail of the inputs and outputs of each stage in Scenario II is similar to Scenario III, except that the silage used in Scenario II is elephant grass, and the silage used in Scenario III is corn. Both scenarios are presented in a single schematic. The consequential approach is used to quantify the envi- ronmental benefits of grid electricity substitution by the pig farm surplus electricity and mineral fertilizer substitution by digestion residue.
iv) Scenario IV: Pig manure substrate and biochar as an additive. Fig. 6 shows the detailed schematic of the steps in this scenario.

13: <header>2.4. Definition of impact categories and choice of LCA method used </header>

Pig manure is composed of the manure itself, urine, feed waste, and water. Its composition depends on different factors such as handling, the technical factors at the livestock farms (pig size, weight, and breed), environmental and dietary factors, etc. Environmental factors are related to temperature and humidity. Animal dietary factors are related to animal feed digestibility, fiber, and vitamin content. The primary pollutants of pig manure are nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and heavy metals such as zinc (Zn) and copper (Cu), as well as pathogenic fecal microorganisms. Improper waste management can seriously impact water, land, and air quality [82]. The production of organic fertilizer compounds (nitrogen and phosphorus) from digestion residue or biofertilizer in the biogas system is for energy crops, 4.3 and 0.48 kg/ton of feedstock, respectively, for nitrogen and phosphorus. Concerning manure, this value for nitrogen is 1.3 kg/ton of feedstock. The biofertilizer is rich in nutrients and can be used as organic fertilizer for cultivation, which would return nutrients to the soil in place of mineral (inorganic) fertilizers [34,49,83.

When pig manure comes in contact with water bodies, it can cause eutrophication due to nutrient accumulation and organic content, cause fish to die, resulting in plant toxicity, modify aquatic biodiversity, and
negatively impact human health, potentially causing worms, allergies, and hepatitis[83].

Another environmental impact of pig production is the emission of gases from urine and pig feces. Ammonium carbamate is a compound in pig manure that can dissociate into carbon dioxide and ammonia. Ammonia can lead to acid rain, resulting in soil and water toxicity. Carbon dioxide is one of the greenhouse gases that can accelerate climate change by intensifying the rise in global earth temperatures and thereby aggravating global warming [84].

Another gas derived from rearing pigs and the anaerobic decompo- sition of manure is methane gas, which has a pollutant potential 27,2 times greater than carbon dioxide [10,11]. Other gases also produced in this process are ammonia, nitrous oxide, and nitrogen, which are all also greenhouse gases [85]. High water consumption is another impact to be considered. Rational water use policies are increasingly important. It is estimated that water consumption per animal per day, considering three production phases, would be approximately 72.9 L for the complete stage, 35.3 L for piglets, and 8.3 L for the finishing stage [86]. It is usually recommended that pig residues be stored in ponds, treated in anaerobic digesters or compost beds, and subsequently sent to be used as biofertilizers for crops [87].

The critical impact categories considered in the co-digestion/ Combined Heat and Power (CHP) generator LCA analysis, i.e., those chosen for this study, are the Abiotic Depletion Potential (ADP), the Acidification Potential (AP), the Eutrophication Potential (EP), the Global Warming Potential (GWP), the Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP), the Photochemical Oxidation Formation Potential (POFP) and the Human Toxicity Potential (HTP).

The Abiotic Depletion Potential assesses impacts related to using or extracting abiotic natural resources, i.e., minerals and fossil fuels or non- renewable resources that will probably be depleted. This category has future impacts due to the scarcity of mineral resources and the lack of mineral options for future generations. This category is expressed in MJ [88] for the abiotic depletion of fossil fuels.

The Acidification Potential refers to emissions of pollutants such as SO2 or NOx into the air. These emissions negatively impact groundwater, surface water, biological organisms, ecosystems, materials, and soil. This impact category is measured in kg SO2 eq. Eutrophication refers to the accumulation of nutrients, such as nitrogen and phosphorus, in the environment, causing accelerated aquatic plant growth. Eutrophication is expressed using kg PO43 [89].

The Global Warming Potential addresses greenhouse gas emissions that contribute to the greenhouse effect over up to 100 years. It is expressed in kg CO2 eq [90]. Ozone Layer Depletion evaluates the impact caused by emitting chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) into the atmo- sphere; these gases result in the depletion of the stratospheric ozone that protects the earth from UV radiation. Human toxicity results from ac- tivities that emit substances that are not biologically degradable and accumulate in living organisms. This can occur by inhaling or digesting these substances [77]. Bio accumulating substances include mercury, platinum, and nickel.

Finally, Photochemical Oxidation is the last impact category evalu- ated and addresses the potential impact of photochemical ozone for- mation from airborne substances. Anthropogenic ozone results from atmospheric reactions between nitrogen oxides and Non-Methane Vol- atile Organic Compounds (NMVOC) [91]. This is expressed in kg C2H4eq.

22: Anaerobic mesophilic bacteria grow best over a specific temperature range. A temperature of 38 °C was considered optimum. Thus, the bio- digester must be heated since the average temperature in the munici- pality is 20 °C. At this temperature, the digestion residue is digested more quickly, reducing the Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) from 53 to 20 days and the required volume of the biodigester by more than 50%.

Fig. 8 shows that Scenario I cannot produce enough electricity to meet the electricity demands at the farm. Pig manure biochar additive is used in Scenario IV, where the energy produced is wholly consumed on the farm. There is higher electricity production in Scenarios II and III compared to Scenarios I and IV, and the farm electricity consumption does not reach 20% of the electricity produced in the co-digestion sys- tem, both for the co-digestion of pig manure with elephant grass silage and for pig manure with corn silage.

The electricity produced in Scenarios II and III is much higher than in Scenarios I and IV due to increased methane yield when co-digestion is implemented.

The installed power are 261 kW, 2,292 kW, 2,362 kW, and 425 kW for Scenarios I, II, III, and IV, respectively, considering 8000 operating hours of the generator set annually and a 25% electricity efficiency conversion from internal combustion engine (ICE) Otto gas cycle and the generator set.

The provisions of Normative Resolution n° 482/2012 [92] and its revision, Normative Resolution n° 687/2015 [93], determines that the active energy generated through the micro or mini-generation distrib- uted is converted into credits with the local distributor, and in the future offset with the electricity consumption from the grid [7,94-96].

Electricity produced through biodigestion is converted into energy credits in the energy compensation system that can be used elsewhere in the future, respecting the limits of the energy distributor. The invest- ment income becomes equivalent to the avoidable consumption costs. Therefore, the enterprise becomes more economically viable and competitive [97].

All the scenarios studied in this work fit into the distributed mini- generation, characterized by the generation of small units injecting surplus production into the distribution network in the installed power range superior to 75 kW and inferior to or equal to 5 MW [98]. Thus, all power ranges fall within the energy compensation system for the distributed mini-generation.
The environmental impact categories for the different scenarios for biogas production via the Life Cycle Assessment were determined and discussed further.

The electricity produced in Scenarios II and III is much higher than in Scenarios I and IV due to increased methane yield when co-digestion is implemented.

The installed power are 261 kW, 2,292 kW, 2,362 kW, and 425 kW for Scenarios I, II, III, and IV, respectively, considering 8000 operating hours of the generator set annually and a 25% electricity efficiency conversion from internal combustion engine (ICE) Otto gas cycle and the generator set.

The provisions of Normative Resolution n° 482/2012 [92] and its revision, Normative Resolution n° 687/2015 [93], determines that the active energy generated through the micro or mini-generation distrib- uted is converted into credits with the local distributor, and in the future offset with the electricity consumption from the grid [7,94-96].

Electricity produced through biodigestion is converted into energy credits in the energy compensation system that can be used elsewhere in the future, respecting the limits of the energy distributor. The invest- ment income becomes equivalent to the avoidable consumption costs. Therefore, the enterprise becomes more economically viable and competitive [97].

All the scenarios studied in this work fit into the distributed mini- generation, characterized by the generation of small units injecting surplus production into the distribution network in the installed power range superior to 75 kW and inferior to or equal to 5 MW [98]. Thus, all power ranges fall within the energy compensation system for the distributed mini-generation.
The environmental impact categories for the different scenarios for biogas production via the Life Cycle Assessment were determined and discussed further.
24: Anaerobic mesophilic bacteria grow best over a specific temperature range. A temperature of 38 °C was considered optimum. Thus, the bio- digester must be heated since the average temperature in the munici- pality is 20 °C. At this temperature, the digestion residue is digested more quickly, reducing the Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) from 53 to 20 days and the required volume of the biodigester by more than 50%.

Fig. 8 shows that Scenario I cannot produce enough electricity to meet the electricity demands at the farm. Pig manure biochar additive is used in Scenario IV, where the energy produced is wholly consumed on the farm. There is higher electricity production in Scenarios II and III compared to Scenarios I and IV, and the farm electricity consumption does not reach 20% of the electricity produced in the co-digestion sys- tem, both for the co-digestion of pig manure with elephant grass silage and for pig manure with corn silage.

GWP is positive for Scenarios I, II, III, and IV. In all scenarios, there are significant contributions and benefits to using mono-digestion and co-digestion from an environmental perspective. This is because generating electricity from biomass consumed on the property or injected back into the grid for later consumption is environmentally beneficial. Digestion residue is used as organic fertilizer, and its nitrogen is a substitute for producing inorganic nitrogen. However, the fugitive emissions of methane gas and the carbon dioxide produced in the bio- digestion and electricity conversion process considerably contribute to GWP emissions.
The transportation stage emissions for the co-digestion scenarios are comparatively low when considering the two stages of the electricity production process, not evident in Fig. 9.

Fig. 10 shows that the GWP values associated with transportation for Scenarios II, III, and IV, can be evaluated by separating the processes and isolating the transportation stage, which is much higher for Scenario IV because it requires a much higher biochar transportation distance than between Oliveira and Divinópolis.

The biogas produced in Scenario I showed the most significant environmental benefits for GWP analysis since it replaces electricity from the grid with the added benefit of having no fossil fuel consumption.
Scenario III presents the most significant contribution to GWP emissions during crop farming due to its higher fossil fuel consumption.

29: Acidification potential is related to the increased acidity in the soil, groundwater, and surface water caused by anthropogenic actions, especially from burning fossil fuels. This is related to NOx, SOx, and NH3 emissions. Fish mortality rates, forests, and degraded building materials are most impacted.

Fig. 11 shows that crop farming processes had the most significant environmental impacts. Transportation-related impacts were minor in all three scenarios (Scenario II, III, and IV), and the anaerobic digestion process mitigated the impacts of acidifying pollutants. Crop farming resulted in pollutants released by diesel-burning agricultural machinery and diesel production. By contrast, excess electricity generated from biogas offsets this, replacing electricity in the grid associated with sulfur monoxide and nitrogen oxide emissions.

Fig. 12 shows the impact of transportation on each scenario. Because of more considerable distances, acidification from transportation emis- sions is higher in Scenario IV. Scenario II resulted in the most significant environmental benefits in the acidification study. The gain from replacing electricity outweighs the environmental liabilities caused by using diesel in agricultural machinery, even when accounting for the
damage caused by the crop treatment.

Eutrophication is caused by the increased application of macronu- trients, such as nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P), to water or soil, causing accelerated aquatic plant growth, spread, and changes to aquatic or terrestrial ecosystems.
Fig. 13 shows that biomass farming results in the most significant eutrophication changes. Scenario III presented values equal to 0.2082 kgPO4 eq/tonSM.

Anaerobic digestion positively impacts these factors by reducing the production of inorganic fertilizers. Also, nitrogen oxide emissions are reduced when electricity consumed from the grid is replaced by elec- tricity produced from biogas in co-digestion. Eutrophication negatively impacts Scenario III, where the potential for reducing anaerobic diges- tion is lower than the impact caused during farming. On the other hand, scenarios I, II, and IV present attractive values. Scenario II had the lowest Eutrophication impacts of all the evaluated scenarios. This is due to anaerobic digestion, which reduces emissions during planting, culti- vation, and harvesting.

The replacement of synthetic nitrogen with organic nitrogen and the electricity from the grid by that produced during anaerobic digestion is not enough to compensate for the diesel emissions during the production of corn silage in Scenario III.

31: Human toxicity occurs when non-biologically degradable substances accumulate in living beings. Some examples of these substances are nickel, platinum, and mercury.
Fig. 14 shows that biomass farming has the most significant pollution potential for human toxicity in Scenarios II and III due to the barium in the diesel fuel used in agricultural machinery.

A positive impact is seen in all scenarios because mineral nitrogen is replaced with organic nitrogen. However, the benefit of replacing mineral nitrogen with organic nitrogen is not enough to offset the impact of the barium content in diesel fuel in Scenarios II and III. As a result, Scenarios II and III are unattractive from a human toxicity perspective. The levels were 40.67 kg1,4-DBeq/tonSM and 96.30 kg1,4- DBeq/tonSM, respectively, even after accounting for the benefits from the digestion/MCI stage. On the other hand, Scenarios I and IV showed harmful human toxicity levels equal to -4.58 kg1,4-DBeq/tonSM and -3.02 kg1,4-DBeg/tonSM, respectively.

1: Photochemical oxidation negatively affects air quality when nitrogen oxides (NOx) react with volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the air. Although this can be expressed as a function of ethane, it is associated with ground-level ozone formation.

Fig. 16 shows that Scenarios II and III had the highest photochemical oxidation values, mainly due to biomass farming. However, the bio- digestion and electricity conversion processes have a significant and negative impact due to fugitive emissions. All scenarios show negative impacts of diesel, carbon monoxide, and methane gas consumption.

The negative impacts of biomass farming are related to diesel con- sumption from tractors and carbon monoxide production (44%). They are also related to producing diesel fuel, carbon monoxide (56%), sulfur monoxide, dioxide, and isopropanol, among other substances.

Scenario I was one of the best Scenarios regarding photochemical oxidation thanks to its process that does not involve the diesel chain. Anaerobic digestion is beneficial because it offsets the consumption of electricity from the central Brazilian grid, composed of energy produced at the risk of increased methane, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and toluene levels. Anaerobic digestion also helps replace nitrogen and
2: Abiotic Depletion is linked to the consumption and reduction of natural fossil resources, especially coal, natural gas, and crude oil reserves.

Fig. 17 shows that Scenarios III and II presented the highest pollution levels due to biomass farming. Environmental benefits, by contrast, are brought about by substituting mineral nitrogen with organic nitrogen. Additionally, there are benefits from using electricity produced from biogas instead of being removed from the grid.

Scenario III was the only Scenario where the environmental benefits of replacing nitrogen, propane, and electricity were insufficient to offset the environmental impacts caused by diesel consumption from farming biomass. Scenario III showed the heaviest environmental impacts and is, therefore, the most harmful Scenario in this category.
3: <header>3.9. Overall comparison between scenarios </header>

The LCA results are presented in Fig. 18 and Fig. 19 along with the seven impact categories considered in this study: Global Warming Po- tential, Acidification Potential, Eutrophication Potential, Human Toxicity Potential, Ozone Layer Depletion Potential, Photochemical Oxidation Potential, and Abiotic Fuel Depletion Potential.

These results are compared from a relative perspective, and impact categories are evaluated according to their environmental impacts using Scenario I as a reference and depending on the benefits or negative
