0: impacts each Scenario brought about. Positive values infer negative impacts, something you want to avoid or generate as little as possible.

No scenario had wholly positive or negative impact results for all categories. However, Scenario I is comparatively the best for all impact categories. Scenario I presents a good relationship in most of the analyzed criteria from a macro perspective of all categories. Scenario III negatively impacts most of the analyzed categories and presents a pos- itive impact only for Ozone Layer Depletion Potential.

From a comparative perspective, Scenario IV presents positive im- pacts only for Acidification Potential. However, its negative impacts are much lower when compared with those from Scenario II and III. Sce- nario IV is always very close to Scenario I due to the similar generation and the amount of additive used, much lower than the amount of sub- strate used in co-digestion.

Although electricity production in Scenarios II and III was close, the amount of diesel consumed for corn crop treatment was higher than for elephant grass. Scenario III had the highest electricity production, yet it is still not viable from an environmental perspective.

Scenarios II and III have higher polluting impacts from biomass farming compared to Scenarios I and IV for most impact categories. On the other hand, the available nitrogen originated as a product, along with the replaced electricity from the grid, results in favorable levels, or

even superior levels, to the Scenarios that did not have treatment stages, such as Scenarios I and IV. Eutrophication Potential for Scenario II and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP) for Scenarios II and III, for example, have positive impacts thanks to electric energy consumption from the grid being offset by electricity produced and mineral nitrogen that can be replaced by organic nitrogen.

In Fig. 18, a radar graph is presented where the highest score is scaled to plus or minus 100% and the lowest is a relative score. As can be seen, Scenario III was the one that presented in absolute values, the worst results in most of the impact categories, followed by Scenario II. Scenario I (base) and Scenario IV present very similar values, with Scenario I presenting the greatest environmental benefits. In Fig. 19 below, the impact categories and comparisons are made compared to the baseline scenario, starting from the base scenario at 0%.

Despite being small (3% of the total), fugitive methane gas emissions and those from a biogenic source significantly impact the ecosystem. Categories such as Global Warming Potential and Photochemical Oxidation Potential are severely affected by fugitive emissions. There- fore, preventing the escape of gases during the process of converting gas into electricity is not only important from an economic perspective but also extremely important from an environmental perspective.
As observed in other studies, pig manure monodigestion has a good

performance from an environmental perspective, with potential avoided in most of the analyzed impact categories [99]. On the other hand, en- ergy production is relatively small compared to co-digestion, as observed in other works [100-102]. Despite the improvement in bio- energy production, the addition of co-substrates showed significant and important impacts in most impact categories, resulting in a more sig- nificant environmental impact [101-104].

Using corn silage as a co-substrate with pig manure showed positive impacts for most impact categories, as also shown in other studies [99]. Fugitive biogas emissions have a substantial impact on the life cycle analysis. Thus, they should be the minimum possible since they affect bioenergy production and severely impact the environment, as seen in other works [105]. The replacement by organic fertilizer has a beneficial role on the environment because it avoids using mineral fertilizers, as also evidenced by other studies [103].

Referring to the Global Warming Potential, it is observed that the most significant beneficial values originate from Base Scenario. This occurs by substituting the electricity consumed from the Brazilian electric energy matrix for the energy from biogas and substituting minerals with organic nitrogen.

Regarding the Acidification Potential, the cultural treatments pre- sented the highest pollutant potential due to the nitrogen oxides emitted in the air in the diesel-burning by the tractor and in diesel production. Substituted electricity from the grid has an environmental benefit, reducing the emissions of nitrogen oxides and sulfur monoxide caused in the production of electricity in the Brazilian energy matrix.

The most significant Eutrophication Potential impacts also occur due to the cultural treatments with the emission of nitrogen oxides in the air with the diesel-burning in the tractor. Environmental benefits come mainly from replacing mineral nitrogen production and from nitrogen oxides avoided when replacing the source of electricity.

With the Human Toxicity Potential, the most significant pollutant potential is present in diesel production in which barium is released. The environmental benefit is mainly due to replacing inorganic nitrogen with nitrogen produced from biodigestion.

Regarding the depletion of the ozone layer, herbicides cause the most significant polluting impact on crop treatment. The environmental benefit is due to the substitution of electricity from the grid and organic nitrogen production, thus avoiding the production of mineral nitrogen.
The Photochemical Oxidation Potential causes the most significant

polluting impact in Scenarios II and III with the emission of carbon monoxide with diesel burning in the tractor and with the emission of carbon monoxide, sulfur monoxide, and other substances with diesel production itself. The benefit comes from replacing grid electricity, inorganic nitrogen, inorganic phosphorus, and liquefied petroleum gas.

Concerning the Abiotic Depletion Potential of fossil fuels, the largest polluter is represented in the cultural treatment by the diesel con- sumption in the tractor since practically all diesel produced comes from crude oil. The environmental benefit is mainly due to replacing mineral nitrogen with organic nitrogen (participation in crude oil, natural gas, and coal) and substituting electricity from the grid.

If, on the one hand, the generation of bioenergy can be significantly increased, on the other hand, an analysis of environmental impacts must be considered since co-substrates and additives have a strong potential for negative results for the greater part of the categories analyzed in this study.
1: impacts each Scenario brought about. Positive values infer negative impacts, something you want to avoid or generate as little as possible.

No scenario had wholly positive or negative impact results for all categories. However, Scenario I is comparatively the best for all impact categories. Scenario I presents a good relationship in most of the analyzed criteria from a macro perspective of all categories. Scenario III negatively impacts most of the analyzed categories and presents a pos- itive impact only for Ozone Layer Depletion Potential.

From a comparative perspective, Scenario IV presents positive im- pacts only for Acidification Potential. However, its negative impacts are much lower when compared with those from Scenario II and III. Sce- nario IV is always very close to Scenario I due to the similar generation and the amount of additive used, much lower than the amount of sub- strate used in co-digestion.

Although electricity production in Scenarios II and III was close, the amount of diesel consumed for corn crop treatment was higher than for elephant grass. Scenario III had the highest electricity production, yet it is still not viable from an environmental perspective.

Scenarios II and III have higher polluting impacts from biomass farming compared to Scenarios I and IV for most impact categories. On the other hand, the available nitrogen originated as a product, along with the replaced electricity from the grid, results in favorable levels, or

even superior levels, to the Scenarios that did not have treatment stages, such as Scenarios I and IV. Eutrophication Potential for Scenario II and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP) for Scenarios II and III, for example, have positive impacts thanks to electric energy consumption from the grid being offset by electricity produced and mineral nitrogen that can be replaced by organic nitrogen.

In Fig. 18, a radar graph is presented where the highest score is scaled to plus or minus 100% and the lowest is a relative score. As can be seen, Scenario III was the one that presented in absolute values, the worst results in most of the impact categories, followed by Scenario II. Scenario I (base) and Scenario IV present very similar values, with Scenario I presenting the greatest environmental benefits. In Fig. 19 below, the impact categories and comparisons are made compared to the baseline scenario, starting from the base scenario at 0%.

Despite being small (3% of the total), fugitive methane gas emissions and those from a biogenic source significantly impact the ecosystem. Categories such as Global Warming Potential and Photochemical Oxidation Potential are severely affected by fugitive emissions. There- fore, preventing the escape of gases during the process of converting gas into electricity is not only important from an economic perspective but also extremely important from an environmental perspective.
As observed in other studies, pig manure monodigestion has a good

performance from an environmental perspective, with potential avoided in most of the analyzed impact categories [99]. On the other hand, en- ergy production is relatively small compared to co-digestion, as observed in other works [100-102]. Despite the improvement in bio- energy production, the addition of co-substrates showed significant and important impacts in most impact categories, resulting in a more sig- nificant environmental impact [101-104].

Using corn silage as a co-substrate with pig manure showed positive impacts for most impact categories, as also shown in other studies [99]. Fugitive biogas emissions have a substantial impact on the life cycle analysis. Thus, they should be the minimum possible since they affect bioenergy production and severely impact the environment, as seen in other works [105]. The replacement by organic fertilizer has a beneficial role on the environment because it avoids using mineral fertilizers, as also evidenced by other studies [103].

Referring to the Global Warming Potential, it is observed that the most significant beneficial values originate from Base Scenario. This occurs by substituting the electricity consumed from the Brazilian electric energy matrix for the energy from biogas and substituting minerals with organic nitrogen.

Regarding the Acidification Potential, the cultural treatments pre- sented the highest pollutant potential due to the nitrogen oxides emitted in the air in the diesel-burning by the tractor and in diesel production. From a comparative perspective, Scenario IV presents positive im- pacts only for Acidification Potential. However, its negative impacts are much lower when compared with those from Scenario II and III. Sce- nario IV is always very close to Scenario I due to the similar generation and the amount of additive used, much lower than the amount of sub- strate used in co-digestion.

Although electricity production in Scenarios II and III was close, the amount of diesel consumed for corn crop treatment was higher than for elephant grass. Scenario III had the highest electricity production, yet it is still not viable from an environmental perspective.

Scenarios II and III have higher polluting impacts from biomass farming compared to Scenarios I and IV for most impact categories. On the other hand, the available nitrogen originated as a product, along with the replaced electricity from the grid, results in favorable levels, or

even superior levels, to the Scenarios that did not have treatment stages, such as Scenarios I and IV. Eutrophication Potential for Scenario II and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP) for Scenarios II and III, for example, have positive impacts thanks to electric energy consumption from the grid being offset by electricity produced and mineral nitrogen that can be replaced by organic nitrogen.

In Fig. 18, a radar graph is presented where the highest score is scaled to plus or minus 100% and the lowest is a relative score. As can be seen, Scenario III was the one that presented in absolute values, the worst results in most of the impact categories, followed by Scenario II. Scenario I (base) and Scenario IV present very similar values, with Scenario I presenting the greatest environmental benefits. In Fig. 19 below, the impact categories and comparisons are made compared to the baseline scenario, starting from the base scenario at 0%.

Despite being small (3% of the total), fugitive methane gas emissions and those from a biogenic source significantly impact the ecosystem. Categories such as Global Warming Potential and Photochemical Oxidation Potential are severely affected by fugitive emissions. There- fore, preventing the escape of gases during the process of converting gas into electricity is not only important from an economic perspective but also extremely important from an environmental perspective.
As observed in other studies, pig manure monodigestion has a good

performance from an environmental perspective, with potential avoided in most of the analyzed impact categories [99]. On the other hand, en- ergy production is relatively small compared to co-digestion, as observed in other works [100-102]. Despite the improvement in bio- energy production, the addition of co-substrates showed significant and important impacts in most impact categories, resulting in a more sig- nificant environmental impact [101-104].

Using corn silage as a co-substrate with pig manure showed positive impacts for most impact categories, as also shown in other studies [99]. Fugitive biogas emissions have a substantial impact on the life cycle analysis. Thus, they should be the minimum possible since they affect bioenergy production and severely impact the environment, as seen in other works [105]. The replacement by organic fertilizer has a beneficial role on the environment because it avoids using mineral fertilizers, as also evidenced by other studies [103].

Referring to the Global Warming Potential, it is observed that the most significant beneficial values originate from Base Scenario. This occurs by substituting the electricity consumed from the Brazilian electric energy matrix for the energy from biogas and substituting minerals with organic nitrogen.

Regarding the Acidification Potential, the cultural treatments pre- sented the highest pollutant potential due to the nitrogen oxides emitted in the air in the diesel-burning by the tractor and in diesel production. Substituted electricity from the grid has an environmental benefit, reducing the emissions of nitrogen oxides and sulfur monoxide caused in the production of electricity in the Brazilian energy matrix.

The most significant Eutrophication Potential impacts also occur due to the cultural treatments with the emission of nitrogen oxides in the air with the diesel-burning in the tractor. Environmental benefits come mainly from replacing mineral nitrogen production and from nitrogen oxides avoided when replacing the source of electricity.

With the Human Toxicity Potential, the most significant pollutant potential is present in diesel production in which barium is released. The environmental benefit is mainly due to replacing inorganic nitrogen with nitrogen produced from biodigestion.

Regarding the depletion of the ozone layer, herbicides cause the most significant polluting impact on crop treatment. The environmental benefit is due to the substitution of electricity from the grid and organic nitrogen production, thus avoiding the production of mineral nitrogen.
The Photochemical Oxidation Potential causes the most significant

polluting impact in Scenarios II and III with the emission of carbon monoxide with diesel burning in the tractor and with the emission of carbon monoxide, sulfur monoxide, and other substances with diesel production itself. The benefit comes from replacing grid electricity, inorganic nitrogen, inorganic phosphorus, and liquefied petroleum gas.

Concerning the Abiotic Depletion Potential of fossil fuels, the largest polluter is represented in the cultural treatment by the diesel con- sumption in the tractor since practically all diesel produced comes from crude oil. The environmental benefit is mainly due to replacing mineral nitrogen with organic nitrogen (participation in crude oil, natural gas, and coal) and substituting electricity from the grid.

If, on the one hand, the generation of bioenergy can be significantly increased, on the other hand, an analysis of environmental impacts must be considered since co-substrates and additives have a strong potential for negative results for the greater part of the categories analyzed in this study.
7: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

The optimal C/N ratios can give a vital reference to determine the minimum and maximum amount of co-substrate usage. In the case of elephant grass silage, the range was between 22.9 and 55.6%, and, in the case of corn silage, the range was 25.5-61.9% in a 20:1-30:1 ratio, respectively. This allows calculating the amount of material to be used, guaranteeing the maximum methane yield.
The midpoint of the optimal C/N ratio is 25/1, 39.3% for elephant grass silage mass and 43.7% for corn silage mass.

Each category's impact from Scenario I can be seen from an LCA analysis and taking Scenario I as a model. Scenario III presented the worst results for the categories Global Warming Potential, Acidification Potential, Eutrophication Potential, Human Toxicity Potential, Photo- chemical Oxidation Formation Potential, and Abiotic Depletion Poten- tial of fossil fuels. Of these, relatively, for human toxicity, Scenario III presents a harmful character, more significant than 2000% when compared to Scenario I. Scenario II appears to be less harmful than Scenario III, but more SO than Scenarios I and IV. Compared to Scenario I, Scenario IV presented the best results for most impact categories.

Despite the gains by grid electricity substitution and the replacement of nitrogen from mineral fertilizers by organic with the use of co- digestion, the use of fossil fuels in planting, harvesting, and silage

production has a strong and negative impact on the environment. Added to this, fugitive methane emissions significantly contribute to altering the patterns of impact categories and making the most biogas-producing scenarios (Scenarios II and III) also those with the most significant environmental impacts. The environmental benefits for Scenarios II and III are smaller than the losses in the final balance. The transport of all scenarios, including charcoal from one municipality to another, did not significantly contribute to the impacts, compared to farming. We believe that to make biogas generation projects viable, more consistent technical, economic, and environmental analysis, laboratory tests to obtain parameters of biogas systems, and more research are necessary to help reduce costs and environmental impacts. Thus, we also believe that there should be greater government involvement in creating public policies that guide research results such as this one. Further research should be conducted to help reduce costs to make biogas generation projects more viable. There must be greater involve- ment on behalf of the government to create public policies and regula- tions that subsidize biogas generation projects for distributed micro and mini generation. New technologies and national and international partnerships can help reduce the costs of domestic and imported equipment and, consequently, increase the number of projects of this type in Brazil. From an environmental perspective, the monodigestion of pig waste for electricity generation presents advantages and environmental ben- efits for most of the impact categories analyzed by the Life Cycle Assessment. However, electricity generation is small compared to co- digestion, in this case corn silage and elephant grass. Concerning the addition of other substrates, if on the one hand, there is an improvement in energy production, on the other, there is a significant worsening of impacts in most categories, leading to a worsening of environmental quality. Fugitive gas emissions strongly impact the life cycle analysis, affecting not only energy generation but also the environment. The use of organic fertilizers contributes to an improvement in environmental quality. Finally, it can be seen that there must always be an environ- mental commitment to increase power generation without significantly compromising the quality of the environment.
